sentences.text: My name was Fritz, F-R-I-T-S, Voorzanger, V-O-O-R-Z-A-N-G-E-R.
I was born in the Hague, the Netherlands, on June 5, 1930.
I'm 68 years old.
Alm-- yeah.
Almost.
My father's name was Salomon.
My biological mother, her name was Elizabeth, and my stepmother, her name was Rosetta.
My grandparents, grandfather, his name was Joseph and my grandmother's name was Heintje, which is Dutch.
I don't know how to translate it.
My brother's name was the Joop, we called him, J-O-O-P.
Or Jope, also a Dutch name.
His official name in the Belgian register was Joseph, but we called him Joop.
No.
After I was born, my father moved to enter Belgium.
He was a diamond cutter, and he had a better opportunity there to make a good living, so he moved the whole family to Belgium.
I don't know which year, but I think it was in 1931.
And I grew up in Belgium, did my schooling there as well.
Most of the family was in Holland.
My father had one brother who lived in Belgium, and he had a large family.
He had nine children.
And he lived close to where we lived, in the same street, actually.
My father had a lot of brothers and sisters.
I think we had about 55, 60 people in our immediate family, but most of them lived in Holland.
Me?
I had contact with my uncle who lived in the same street with us, because it was a large family.
And my mother sent me over there because I wouldn't eat at home.
I would always eat at the other places.
But the family in Holland, I don't recall too much of that, because we didn't see them that often.

My memories is very minimal on that because I was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] born with a lung problem and I was always being sent out either to the woods or the mountains or to the ocean air, so I didn't have much of a family life.
But the things I remember with my father-- he was a good man, hard worker.
And very strict.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very strict.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My biological mother, I don't remember anything about, because she passed away when I was about three or four years old.
So I only remember my stepmother.
My father got remarried in 1937, and I always regarded her as my real mother, of course, because I didn't know anything else.
Yes, I do.
I have photos of my father and my biological mother and also of my stepmother.
And some photos of my aunts and uncles.
We spoke Dutch.
No, we did not practice any, anything.
We didn't go to shul or anything like that.
I know that my father did not eat pork, that he refused to have in the house.
And that was about the extent of it.
Not when I was a little boy, no.
But came later when I went to school that people started to yell at me.
And the neighbors especially when the war started in 1940-- that's when the neighbors started to call us names.
That's when I realized it, that I was Jewish, more than before the war.
Because the Germans, they came right away with a decree that we had to star-- we had to wear the Star of David.
So it was an open thing.
Right.
Right.
What sort of schools did you go to as a young child?
First I went to kindergarten school in Antwerp.
Actually, the name of the city was Bergen.
And after that I went to public school.
And I attended four grades at the public school until the war broke out, and my father moved me to another school in the city of Antwerp, because he thought the education was better there for me.
But I only stayed there for about one year, and that's when my parents were deported.
I didn't go to school until after-- way after the war.
But when you were a young child, would you say that you came from a wealthy home or a middle-class home?
Middle-class.
Middle class.
Yeah, my father was-- he made god money.
He was a diamond cutter.
We had food, and we had clothing, entertainment.
We had everything that a child should want to have.
What kind of a child I was?
I had a hard time eating.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember my-- always my mother-- always kept yelling at me, Jack, chew.
Kept food in my mouth.
I was never eating.
I was skinny, and they were worried about me.
And that's why they sent me out to all these places, you know, so people could take care of me and give me constant supervision, because they couldn't handle it.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My father was a very strict man, and he had a pair of hands, which, when he put them on you, that hurt.
But he forgot right away.
When he hit you, that was it.
The punishment was done.
Childhood, what is-- how long is a childhood?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think the happiest years of my life in my childhood was when, when my father got remarried in 1937 until the war broke out in 1940-- three years.
That's, that's when I was happy, yeah.
The first time I was home all the time.
I didn't have to be sent out.
I had a mother who was taking care of me, because my mother was ill.
And I was always being taken care of by other people, so I didn't have a home actually.
So that's the first time I had a home.
And I had a mother, and  I had a father, and I had a brother.
So that was-- those were my happiest years, those three years.
The thing I remember vividly is that my father was listening to the radio every night.
And he was always listening to Hitler's speeches.
And he was very worried about it.
I don't know exactly what the conversation was, but I know that he was worried about it.
And he knew something was going to happen.
But I was not aware of that, of course.
I was not part of that.
I was too young.
No.
Because the school I went to in-- that was in 1941 or 1940.
That was primarily Jewish boys who went to school there, because there was a very big Jewish neighborhood there, very religious Jews.
And we went to that school, so the was not that much antisemitism in the school itself.
But of course, gradually they, they disappeared [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until I finally had to go away to school myself in 1943-- or '42, rather.
They were being round up and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] transported off to concentration camps.
No, I was not.
I asked my father.
I said, you know, something-- I said, you know, so-and-so didn't come to school today, and so-and-so I haven't seen for about a week.
And my father said, yes.
He said, you know, because the Germans are gathering them, and they are putting them away.
And it'll be all over when the war is over.
So that's, that's the way I accepted it.
I didn't make anything out of it.
No, not really.
I don't think I had a particular feeling.
I just wanted to get my studies done.
And my father was out of work at the time, couldn't work, so I was stealing.
I was stealing wood to put in the stove to burn.
Sometimes I would steal food, go to the grocery store and steal apples or potatoes or something like that.
But besides that, the, the antisemitism didn't affect me that much.
That I couldn't tell you.
I think they kept it mostly hidden from me.
Because my father was not working.
He could not work.
He was a Jew.
And we had no food at the house.

We had no coal to burn in the stove, so I went to a little place outside Antwerp where they had a depot of wood and I would schlep the wood home and my father would go in the basement and he would cut it up in small pieces and burn it.
And there was a funny thing I remember.
That at one time he was standing around the stove with my mother and warming his hands.
And he was singing an aria from 'La Boheme.' [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember that.
I was aware only in the fact that we were [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] forced to wear the Star of David and that we were not allowed to ride the street cars.
And we had to be off the streets before dark.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Besides that, I don't remember actually what the restrictions-- there were more restrictions, I'm sure, but I don't know which they were because my father never talked about it.
We had to.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was a scared man, and he did whatever they told him to do.
No, not at that time.
That came much later.
By 1942, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my father was telling my mother that his friends and also some of the relatives were being picked up by the Germans to be transported to forced labor camps.
And he told her that this may happen to him, too, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and his main worry was my brother and me, that we, we should be taken care of while he was not there.
So he arranged to have my brother in a preventorium in a little village outside Antwerp, Westmalle, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who were taking care of him.
And he arranged for me to be sent to a camp outside Antwerp with a bunch of other children.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]
When my brother left to go to that preventorium was in May 1942.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father received a notice from the German Wehrmacht to report on a Saturday morning at the central station in Antwerp for forced labor camp.
And he went.
He packed his suitcase and he went.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then that's when my mother decided that it was time for me to go out.
And she sent me to that camp outside Antwerp where I was with the children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they sent me home through August, I think it was, the middle of August.
They sent me home because the school was starting.
The school year was starting, so the kids had to go back to school.
And then I came home, and three days later they came in-- the Gestapo came in, and they picked up my mother and me.
Yes.
He had told his friends that he was going to report.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they came over to the house on the Saturday morning, and they all came and they brought some food for him to take.
There was one very close friend of my father.
As a matter of fact, her daughter and husband, they live here in the United States, in Century City.
She came with a couple of pots of jam for him to take with him.
Other people brought warm clothing.
So we had a huge suitcase all packed.

[PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS] The thing that I remember most is that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when my father said goodbye, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] my mother was hanging on his coat, [SOBBING] asking him not to go.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So they had to restrain her, and then he just closed the door and left.
I never saw him again.
He thought he was going to a-- to a camp to work.
That's what he thought.
That's what he told all the Jews there.
You're going to go to a camp, and you're going to be working.
And you're going to have housing, and you're going to have good food.
You're going to have everything.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So he was just going to work, that's what he thought he was going to do.
And they told him also in a letter to bring warm clothing.
I was 12.
My brother was 18, and he was in, in that preventorium.
So he wasn't there when this happened.
Right, yes.
It was still dark, it was around 5 o'clock in the morning, and somebody knocked on the door.
And my mother opened the door and we heard the voice.
It was actually-- it was a Belgian policeman who came knocking on the door and-- to tell us to pack and assemble in the street.
And my mother must have known this was going to eventually happen because she had our suitcases packed already.
She was ready.
All she had to do was get dressed.
And she dressed me, and we took the suitcases and we went onto the street.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the whole street-- it was a whole Jewish neighborhood, so we had all the people standing outside in front of their homes with their suitcases.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they called out their names.
And the Germans, as meticulous as they tried to be, they called the names off in alphabetical order.
And every time they called off a name, those people, they were ordered to go into a truck.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course my name being Voorzanger, with a V, I was one of the last ones in the last truck.
So my mother and I, we went into the truck.
And when the truck started to roll-- it was rolling very slowly-- my mother told me to jump.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I didn't want to jump.
She got mad at me, and she told me to listen to her.
And she told me, I want you to jump right now.
And so I jumped.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
And I ran.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had no idea where to go.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] So I went home and there was nobody home because everybody in the house was gone.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
So I went out again, and I tried to find out where they took the truck.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I found out that they had taken the truck to a school, and they had rounded up all the Jews on the playground.
And the school was-- the entrance to the school was being guarded by German soldiers.
And I sneaked by the guards.
[PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS]
And I found my mother sitting on the suitcase, crying.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when she saw me, she was furious, and she told me to leave her immediately.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I left.
And I sneaked past the guards again.
I sneaked out of the school.
And I went to some friends of my parents.
And I knocked on their door, and they took me in.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they were-- they were not Jewish people, but they were very close friends with my brother and my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she had connections with the underground, and they contacted the underground.
And somehow they were able to tell my brother where I was.
And he came the next day, and he picked me up.
And he took me with him to the preventorium [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Westmalle.
Westmalle is about-- it's a little village 24 kilometers out of Antwerp.
A little tiny village, it's a beautiful little city with woods.
He was being-- he was being protected there because it was a preventorium.
And there was-- what I understand, there was one thing that the Germans were afraid of, and that was to catch tuberculosis.
Well this was to prevent it.
And so they never bothered to, to come in and check.
So this was kind of a safe heaven, or haven.
There was another Jewish boy there who was hiding there as well.
Somehow [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] somebody found out that my brother was hiding there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they told him-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this all-- and they thought it was maybe better that he should leave the preventorium.
Because they didn't want to put me and the other boy in jeopardy.
So they hid him in their house.
In the-- the man who ran the place, in his house.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And sometime later after that, my brother got another letter in the mail to report immediately [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to a forced labor camp.
And he left.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But somehow-- I don't know exactly what happened.
But I think he escaped or something.
Because he was able to survive in Germany without being in a camp.
No, I don't know.
He doesn't like to talk about it.
